meta.maintainer_note: Review facts and links against ai.meta.com/blog and github.com/facebookresearch/tribev2 at least every 14 days.

facts.title: A tri-modal digital twin for brain response
facts.body: Meta FAIR’s TRIBE v2 predicts high-resolution fMRI-style activity from sight, sound, or language — so researchers can stress-test hypotheses before every study needs a scanner slot.

facts.title: Trained on 700+ volunteers, many hours of natural media
facts.body: The model learns from a large cohort watching and listening to diverse real-world images, podcasts, video, and text — not just tidy lab sentences.

facts.title: Far finer brain coverage than earlier public baselines
facts.body: Meta reports roughly a 70× leap in effective prediction resolution versus comparable prior models — enough surface detail to compare cortical patterns meaningfully.

facts.title: Built on Algonauts-class encoding science
facts.body: The stack extends ideas from Meta’s Algonauts 2025 competition work — encoding models that map stimuli to brain responses — then scales data and capacity.

facts.title: Weights, code, and paper under CC BY-NC
facts.body: Researchers get Hugging Face weights, GitHub training/inference code, and the full paper to reproduce and extend — commercial use needs a separate license.

facts.title: Official interactive demo
facts.body: Meta ships a browser demo to explore TRIBE v2 on your own clips — useful context for what “population average” brain maps can and can’t claim.

facts.title: Foundation model across vision, audition, language
facts.body: The publication frames TRIBE as one model for in-silico neuroscience: shared representations for how humans process complex, real-world stimuli.

facts.title: Brain Diff ≠ a clinical scan
facts.body: You’re seeing population-average cortical contrast from TRIBE v2 after text is run through a speech-like pathway — insightful for messaging, not a diagnosis.
